artifacts.content:
Em,

I’m still wired from today and needed to get this out of my head a bit before I try to sleep.

The woman who slipped this afternoon – her name’s Laura Jenkins – went down around 2:35 in the main front aisle, right by the seasonal display. The awful part is that I was literally in the back office on that long call with Mark Delaney about the possible IRS payroll audit when it happened. We were going over those 2023 payroll tax discrepancies line by line, and I stayed on with him until about 2:40. If I hadn’t been tied up trying to untangle that mess, I probably would’ve done my usual 2:15 walkthrough and seen the paint that dripped near the display.

I keep replaying it: I told Evan to stay up front on the register because we’re still short after letting Tyler Grant go last week for showing up hungover again – remember the December 28 incident when he came in visibly impaired? With his shifts gone, we were already stretched thin. So Evan didn’t do his usual sweep, I didn’t do mine because of the Mark call, and Laura walked right into that light off‑white paint on the concrete.

It’s like all the weak spots hit at once – the tax stuff with Mark, the staffing hole from firing Tyler, and then this accident. I keep thinking about Dad and how he’d handle it, and I just feel like I’m dropping plates in every direction.

Kids okay tonight? How’s Sophie after practice? I’ll try not to wake you when I finally come up.

– Dan

Hannah,

Catching up between customers and thought I’d answer your last message, and also explain why I sounded so out of it on the phone the other night.

The customer who fell at the store on January 15 – Laura Jenkins – went down right while I was in the office talking to Mom about arranging in‑home care after her mild stroke at Providence St. Vincent earlier this month. I’d planned my usual 2:00 PM floor inspection, but Mom called and we got into details about a home health aide schedule and whether she can stay in the Beaverton duplex. I ended up on the phone from about 2:10 to 2:35, in that tiny office on Maple Ridge, instead of walking the aisles. That’s exactly when Laura stepped on the off‑white paint in the front aisle and slipped.

What makes it weirder is that Emily reminded me later that she actually treated Laura once back in May 2020 at Providence Portland, in OT, right before she cut back to part‑time because of burnout. And Mom realized Laura was also in that fall‑risk group she did at the Providence outpatient wing back in October 2021 after her first little stroke. Tiny city, I guess.

So now we’ve got Mom’s health to juggle, Emily’s hours at the hospital, Sophie’s soccer and Caleb’s robotics, and this accident hanging over the business. I keep thinking if I’d just cut that call with Mom shorter and done the walkthrough on time, none of this would have happened.

Anyway, how are you and Mike doing in Seattle? Kids still obsessed with Minecraft? Tell them Uncle Dan will trade them a birdhouse for some redstone tips.

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Mom, the store, and that customer

artifacts.content:
Personal journal – 2026-01-18

I called Laura Jenkins today to check in. Hearing her walk through everything made my stomach knot up again.

She says the left hip and lower back are still pretty bad. The ER at Providence told her it was a hip contusion and lumbar strain, no fracture, but the pain is aggravating the chronic sciatica she’s had since that 2019 car accident on I‑5 near Salem. She’s been seeing Dr. Laura Kim at Providence Portland for that – steroid injections and low‑dose opioids – and now she’s anxious about asking for an early refill in early February. She actually said, “I don’t want my primary thinking I’m drug‑seeking because of your store.” That one hurt.

On top of that, the wrist sprain is messing with the carpal tunnel her occupational health doc, Dr. Aaron Patel at Rose City Accounting Group, documented after all that unpaid overtime typing tax returns in late 2024. She’s worried HR will label everything “pre‑existing” if she asks for more modified duties or ever needs workers’ comp.

She also told me the pain and being off her feet are wrecking her diabetes routine. She’s on metformin and weekly Ozempic with Dr. Nguyen at the Providence diabetes clinic since March 2023. Missing her Eastside Community Center gym sessions has pushed her fasting blood sugar back into the 180–200 range, and she’s gained about 8 pounds since Thanksgiving from stress‑eating. She sounded embarrassed admitting that.

None of this changes the fact that she slipped on paint in my aisle at 2:35 PM. But it does make everything feel heavier – like my one missed walkthrough is tangled up with years of her health issues.

Mom,

I know I’ve already given you the basic version over the phone, but I want you to have the fuller picture since you’ve lived through every up and down of this store with me and Dad.

The woman who fell is named Laura Jenkins. She’s the same lady who accused Sophie of scratching her car in our parking lot back in August 2022 at 4127 Maple Ridge. Remember how upset Soph was? I pulled the security footage and it showed she hadn’t even left the store then. Emily followed up with Laura by phone and even from her Providence Portland email to clear it up. Things were cordial but always a little strained when she came in after that.

Now Laura’s back in our lives in the worst way. Her attorney, Mark Holloway, sent a pretty stern letter on January 19. He even referenced that old trip‑and‑fall case we settled in 2023 with the delivery driver, Carlos Mendoza – the one on the icy loading dock out back that ended in that $18,000 confidential mediation on November 2, 2023. I hate that there’s a pattern here: first Mendoza and the ice, then the O’Donnell near‑fall last March 11 with the melted snow by the entrance (her son Patrick tried to squeeze us for $7,500), and now this paint spill with Laura.

All I can think about is Dad’s photo behind the counter and how proud he was that people trusted Thompson Hardware. I’m terrified of losing that.

On the home front, Sophie’s still loving soccer and Caleb wants to build another birdhouse next weekend, so life keeps rolling.

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: More details about the fall at the store

artifacts.content:
Business notebook – 2026-01-16

Trying to get my arms around the fallout from Laura Jenkins’ fall and what it means for the shop.

Property side first: the damage to the seasonal display wasn’t huge – a handful of EverBright LED light boxes with crushed corners and one chipped ceramic lantern. What bothered me more was going through that stuff and seeing the dried blood smudged on a few boxes and the lantern from where Laura braced herself. Emily said at Providence they treat anything from immunosuppressed patients as higher‑risk biohazard, and Laura mentioned being on a biologic that suppresses her immune system. So I followed Emily’s infection‑control routine from her OT unit: gloves, double‑bagging in those red liners she brought home, and straight to discard instead of the discount bin.

The cracked plastic price‑tag holder she grabbed was easy to replace from spares – cost under five bucks – but that bracket was part of the last shelving unit Dad installed before the 2018 break‑in when they stole his old cash drawer and some of his tools. I marked it in my own notes as “emotional value – Dad’s last fixture,” even though the adjuster will only care that it’s a cheap piece of plastic.

Floor cleaned up fine with the remover Emily picked up after her shift. No obvious etching on the polished concrete.

Separate note: the spilled paint was from a custom off‑white batch we’d mixed back on October 18, 2025 for Mom’s Beaverton duplex (the one with the 2023 leak and mold). I kept the damaged can and snapped photos of the label and batch number for her landlord file, just in case.

metadata.title: Damage and cleanup notes – Laura J. fall

artifacts.content:
Mike,

You asked how things were really going, so here’s the unfiltered version while I sit here with the store coffee machine wheezing in the background.

Laura Jenkins’ lawyer sent an email on the 20th, and between that and the old Lang ladder judgment from June 18, 2024 (we’re still paying that $4,750 off at $250 a month), I’m starting to feel like we’ve got a target on our backs. I met with Lisa McCormick at the Umpqua Bank branch on SE Division this morning (1/22) to talk about bumping our line of credit from $75k to $125k. I told her straight: if this claim and another potential one both go badly, we might have to miss a quarterly estimated tax payment and push Sophie’s spring 2026 soccer academy fees.

And then there’s the internal stuff I can’t say out loud to many people. On the 16th, in the break room, Tyler Briggs quietly admitted he’d been vaping THC in his car over lunch before coming back on the 15th – the day of the spill. He’s worried his slowed reaction time is part of why the paint sat there so long. Between that, firing Tyler Grant after he showed up visibly impaired on December 28, and the general staffing scramble, I feel like I’m running a circus, not a hardware store.

Meanwhile, Caleb just wants help with his Lego robotics project, and Sophie’s stressing about tryouts. Emily and I keep talking about that long‑promised Oregon coast road trip, but right now it feels like fantasy.

Anyway, how’s Seattle? Still buried in code and rain?

Dan

Event: Sunday catch‑up with Mom and errands

Time: Sunday 2026-01-25, 11:30 AM – 4:00 PM
Location: Start at Mom’s duplex in Beaverton; then errands around SE Portland

Notes:

– 11:30 AM – 1:00 PM: Visit Mom (Maggie) at the Beaverton duplex. Bring the photos of the custom off‑white paint can from the October 18, 2025 batch we mixed at Thompson Hardware for her unit with the 2023 plumbing leak/mold issues. She wants them for her landlord’s insurance file. Also talk through the in‑home care schedule again after her mild stroke at Providence St. Vincent earlier this month; see how she’s feeling about maybe moving closer.

– Quick reminder her fall‑risk group at Providence Portland back in October 2021 is where she first met Laura Jenkins – weird that Laura’s now the one who slipped on our paint spill on 1/15 at 2:35 PM.

– 1:15 – 2:00 PM: Swing by Eastside Community Center to renew my lap swim pass and pick up a brochure for Laura about lower‑impact classes (only if it doesn’t feel intrusive to offer later; she mentioned missing her gym routine and her fasting sugars creeping into the 180–200 range when we talked on 1/18).

– 2:15 – 3:00 PM: Grocery run (Trader Joe’s on SE 39th). Get stuff for Sophie’s lunches and Caleb’s birdhouse‑building snacks.

– 3:15 – 4:00 PM: Call Emily on her break at Providence Portland to check how her part‑time OT schedule is going and whether she’s still thinking about cutting another shift.
